edge: Computer vision and AI are key advancements enabling the growth in demand for cloud-based visual analytics.
In cloud-based visual analytics, images from a low-powered edge device are transmitted to the cloud for analysis.
The use of conventional codecs for compression introduces artifacts that degrade downstream analytic tasks.
Split-DNN computing partitions a DNN into client-side and server-side portions to address the challenges of cloud-based visual analytics.
Bottleneck units, as low-complexity neural networks, are introduced at the split point to transform intermediate layer features for compression and transmission.
Optimizing the pipeline for compression and task-performance requires high-quality estimates of the information-theoretic rate of intermediate features.
Most works on compression for image analytics use heuristic approaches to estimate the rate, resulting in suboptimal performance.
A high-quality neural rate-estimator is proposed to provide accurate estimates of the information-theoretic rate for compression in image analytics.
The lower-dimensional bottleneck output is interpreted as a latent representation of the intermediate features, optimizing the rate-distortion problem.
The rate-distortion optimization problem is addressed through training an equivalent variational auto-encoder with an appropriate loss function.
The training of a variational auto-encoder involves an appropriate loss function to improve rate-distortion outcomes.
Replacing supervised loss terms with distillation-based losses in a teacher-student framework allows for unsupervised training of bottleneck units without explicit training labels.
The demonstrated method outperforms several state-of-the-art methods by obtaining improved task accuracy at lower bitrates on image classification and semantic segmentation tasks.
Visual analytics powered by computer-vision and AI are increasingly being deployed in various domains like retail, industry 4.0, security, and smart-cities.
Deep-neural networks that are too complex to be implemented on low-powered mobile or client devices are being used for visual analytics.
Visual data captured by a mobile device is transmitted over the network to a server or the cloud, and data-compression techniques are applied to manage the data-rate.
Standard data compression techniques like JPEG, BPG, H.264, H.265, etc. are suboptimal for visual analytics since they optimize rate-distortion performance for human perception.
Visual analytics is known to be suboptimal for optimizing rate-distortion performance, which is more focused on human perception rather than semantics-based analytics.
Visual analytics is suboptimal for semantics-based analytics since it optimizes rate-distortion performance based on human perception.
Task performance can degrade severely in the presence of even mild compression artifacts.
DNN-computing, also known as collaborative intelligence, has emerged as a recent paradigm.
DNN-computing involves partitioning a DNN into front-end and back-end, with specially designed neural networks like bottleneck layers introduced.
DNN-computing involves the use of bottleneck layers to transform high-dimensional intermediate features into a lower-dimensional space for greater compression.
Early approaches to split-computing explored the use of simple lossless and lossy compression techniques to compress the intermediate features.
Interactions between intermediate features and lossless techniques resulted in only mild reduction in bandwidth.
Naively quantizing the intermediate features during inference to reduce bitrate leads to a drop in the task performance.

Inspired by the impressive results of ML-based image compression approaches, subsequent approaches include the quantization operation in the end-to-end training of the model, yielding significantly better rate-distortion performance.
Approaches yield significantly better rate-distortion performance than that obtained by traditional image compression methods.

The parameters of the trained DNN model are valid only for a particular split-point and for a particular compression level.
Changing either the split point or the compression level requires a complete retraining of the entire model, increasing training complexity significantly.

The design and training of bottleneck units follow the approach of Datta et al.
Inspired by methods in NAS, the procedure involves exploring architectural hyper-parameters and training hyper-parameters.

The neck encoder mentioned in the context is associated with hyper-parameters, particularly the number of channels and stride of convolutional kernels used. These hyper-parameters are crucial in the bottleneck encoder design.

The training hyper-parameter space includes variables such as weight (Lagrange multipliers) λ in the training loss function. This weight parameter plays a significant role in training the bottleneck unit.
Within the training hyper-parameter space, the quantization step size, Q, is utilized to discretize the latent space. This step size affects the training process of the bottleneck unit.

During training, a bottleneck unit is utilized with fixed topology and specific hyper-parameters for training. This training process focuses on adjusting the weights of the bottleneck unit without impacting the original network weights.

The bottleneck layer is evaluated based on its accuracy and the bit-rate required to transmit compressed features. This evaluation helps in determining the effectiveness of the bottleneck layer in achieving desired performance metrics.

The Pareto optimal set of points is derived from the trained bottleneck candidates. These points represent the solutions that achieve the best trade-off between accuracy and bit-rate, making them highly efficient in the context of bottleneck design.
The points lying on the Pareto frontier correspond to the set of trained bottleneck layers that yield the optimal accuracy vs compression performance.
The trained bottleneck layers yield the optimal accuracy vs compression performance.
In the split DNN architecture, the intermediate features from the final layer at the front-end are compressed and transmitted to the back-end.
A specially designed bottleneck unit is introduced at the split point.

The bottleneck encoder transforms the output of the front-end into a lower-dimensional space more suited for compression, and the bottleneck decoder restores the lower-dimensional features into the original higher dimensional space.

The experimental approach is tested on two analytic tasks: image classification and semantic segmentation. Image classification is tested on the ImageNet dataset using a Resnet50 model, while semantic segmentation is tested on the MS-COCO 2017 dataset using a DeepLab v3 model.

Different DNN models are used for image classification and semantic segmentation tasks. The images are scaled to different resolutions for each task, 224x224 for classification and 513x513 for segmentation.
Details about the training parameters such as learning rates and schedulers are provided in the supplementary material for further reference by the interested reader.

The approach presented enhances the performance of split-computing models for image analytics.
Improved modeling of the rate-function via a neural rate-estimator improves the rate-distortion performance.
The framework can easily be extended for unsupervised training of bottleneck layers by replacing a supervisory loss with a distillation-based loss.
